text:
Before setting up a governance structure, first we should describe what kind of problems it supposed to solve. Different problems require different structures.

I think we don't have much problems yet that need advanced governance structure. I'm fine with current. Simple is better.

He is quite prolific and covers quite a range from programming UX through to experimental PL design practices and musical live coding. A few starting points:
Blackwell, A., Collins, N., 2005. The programming language as a musical instrument. Proceedings of PPIG05 (Psychology of Programming Interest Group) 3, 284–289.
Blackwell, A., Green, T., 2003. Notational systems–the cognitive dimensions of notations framework. HCI Models, Theories, and Frameworks: Toward an Interdisciplinary Science. Morgan Kaufmann.
Blackwell, A.F., 2006a. Ten years of cognitive dimensions in visual languages and computing. Journal of Visual Languages and Computing 4, 285–287.
Blackwell, A.F., 2006b. The reification of metaphor as a design tool. ACM Transactions on Computer-Human Interaction (TOCHI) 13, 490–530.
Blackwell, A.F., Church, L., Green, T.R.G., 2008. The abstract is’ an enemy’: Alternative perspectives to computational thinking, in: Proceedings PPIG. pp. 34–43.

He is quite prolific and covers quite a range from programming UX through to experimental PL design practices and musical live coding. A few starting points:
Blackwell, A., Collins, N., 2005. The programming language as a musical instrument. Proceedings of PPIG05 (Psychology of Programming Interest Group) 3, 284–289.

And this is really interesting:
Edge, D., Blackwell, A., 2006. Correlates of the cognitive dimensions for tangible user interface. Journal of Visual Languages & Computing, Ten Years of Cognitive Dimensions 17, 366–394. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jvlc.2006.04.005>

And this is really interesting:
Edge, D., Blackwell, A., 2006. Correlates of the cognitive dimensions for tangible user interface. Journal of Visual Languages & Computing, Ten Years of Cognitive Dimensions 17, 366–394. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jvlc.2006.04.005>
His co-author Luke Church is active in these circles, and <@UEH6T3RJB> has also co-authored some articles with him

These frameworks are a really helpful way to structure thinking around the topic.

One that I've found useful is Daniel Moody's ' The “Physics” of Notations: Towards a Scientific Basis for Constructing Visual  Notations in Software Engineering_  <http://www.cs.uu.nl/docs/vakken/arm/literature/Moody2009-DesignScience.pdf>

while trying to find the PDF for this again, I came across a paper looking at using it directly for visual programming language analysis: _Towards an Operationalization of the “Physics of Notations” for the Analysis of Visual Languages_ (Storrle & Fish) <https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/d4bc/b466b1390295354d0cc897ab02058f2f8cad.pdf>

These frameworks are a really helpful way to structure thinking around the topic.

One that I've found useful is Daniel Moody's ' The “Physics” of Notations: Towards a Scientific Basis for Constructing Visual  Notations in Software Engineering_  <http://www.cs.uu.nl/docs/vakken/arm/literature/Moody2009-DesignScience.pdf>

while trying to find the PDF for this again, I came across a paper looking at using it directly for visual programming language analysis: _Towards an Operationalization of the “Physics of Notations” for the Analysis of Visual Languages_ (Storrle & Fish) <https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/d4bc/b466b1390295354d0cc897ab02058f2f8cad.pdf>
if I remember correctly, physics of notations (PoN) is slightly more prescriptive than cognitive dimensions (CD)
if I remember correctly, physics of notations (PoN) is slightly more prescriptive than cognitive dimensions (CD)

Quoting PoN:
_"The  Physics  of  Notations  incorporates  both  a  Type  IV  theory  (Section  3)  and  a  Type  V  theory  (Section  4),  which  are  higher  evolutionary forms than the CDs framework (Type I). However it should not be seen as a direct competitor for the CDs frame-work as its scope is much more modest (visual notations rather than  cognitive  artifacts).  Instead,  it  should  be  seen  as  complementary:  it  provides  exactly  the  type  of  detailed,  domain-specific analysis that the authors of the CDs framework argued was  necessary  to  supplement  the  “broad  brush”  analysis  provided  by  CDs"_

Type 1: Theory for analyzing
Type 4: Theory for predicting and explaining
Type 5: Theory for design and action

Haha good catch! I am working on a history of early software engineering / programming practices (c 1947-1959 ish) from the Whirlwind / SAGE project. I like thinking about the future and past of programming at the same time.

Haha I love that this was an option on the poll. I spent 5 years trying to build a commercially viable visual programming environment (<http://dropsource.com|dropsource.com>). 

Eventually I came to the conclusion that code is a damn good representation (<https://aidancunniffe.com/why-programming-is-hard-to-fundamentally-improve-4101612d4ad9>). I’m focusing on building a smart programming assistant that can help with the dirty and repetitive parts of code so we can have the best of both worlds. It’s changed a lot since we went through YC this summer but I’m excited to show some updates in early ‘19

attachments.title: Why Programming is Hard to Fundamentally Improve – Spare Thoughts By Aidan Cunniffe
attachments.fallback: Spare Thoughts By Aidan Cunniffe: Why Programming is Hard to Fundamentally Improve – Spare Thoughts By Aidan Cunniffe

Honestly I don't really know what I'm doing but I'm disappointed with the current state of affairs across many aspects in the field of computing and programming. At this point I'm just working through ideas - reading, thinking, discussing and writing. My vague/broad goal is to find "fundamental ideas and powerful abstractions to organize, program and use computer systems."  Some of my thoughts are posted here: <http://shalabh.com/programmable-systems/|shalabh.com/programmable-systems/>

I too am frustrated with the current state of affairs (especially how much “running in place” is required to keep a user-facing program working over the course of years). Right now I’m studying long term data storage as one angle into this, and David Rosenthal’s blog has been very interesting in that respect: <https://blog.dshr.org/>

attachments.title: DSHR's Blog
attachments.text: I'm David Rosenthal, and this is a place to discuss the work I'm doing in Digital Preservation.
